metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Acknowledge that `stage_and_commit` has no multi-event batch API and that the two writes (SIGNATURE + ENQUEUE_ENRICH) are necessarily sequential under two separate lock acquisitions; promote the drain's implicit-enqueue fallback as the primary correctness mechanism, not a secondary one.
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'Enqueue is written in the SAME locked batch as the certification/attestation write (atomic — no certified-but-unqueued gap)'. However, `stage_and_commit` (the only write-path entry point) takes a single event per call and acquires `write_lock` once per event — there is no multi-event batch API. Two sequential calls to `stage_and_commit` acquire the lock twice, creating a window between the SIGNATURE write and the ENQUEUE_ENRICH write where a crash leaves the ticket certified but unenqueued. The plan notes a fallback ('if batching is impossible, the drain treats...') but the batching premise is not supported by the existing API.
findings.location: What section: 'Enqueue is written in the SAME locked batch as the certification/attestation write (atomic — no certified-but-unqueued gap; if batching is impossible...)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either name the new batch-write mechanism (e.g. a txn-style inline lock wrapper in `_store/event_append.py` or `_commands/txn.py`) as an explicit edit target in Scope, or acknowledge that the 'same locked batch' invariant relies exclusively on the drain's implicit-enqueueable fallback and remove the atomicity claim.
findings.finding: The plan claims the `ENQUEUE_ENRICH` event is written 'in the SAME locked batch as the certification/attestation write (atomic)'. However, the existing signing path (`sign_plan_review` → `signing.sign_manifest` → `append_event`) acquires its own per-call `write_lock` inside `write_and_push`. There is no current multi-event batch mechanism in the cert path — the claim that both events land in one lock window requires new infrastructure (a txn-style inline lock, as in `txn.py`) that the plan does not name as an edit target. The fallback ('if batching is impossible, drain treats certified-with-stale/no-digest as implicitly enqueueable') may therefore always apply.
findings.location: What section: 'Enqueue is written in the SAME locked batch as the certification/attestation write (atomic — no certified-but-unqueued gap; if batching is impossible…)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the atomicity claim: since no multi-event batch write API exists, revise the primary mechanism to 'append ENQUEUE_ENRICH immediately after the SIGNATURE commit as a best-effort second write; the drain's implicit-enqueable fallback (certified+stale/no-enqueue) is the gap-safety net, not the exception path.'
findings.finding: The plan's primary mechanism states ENQUEUE_ENRICH is written 'in the SAME locked batch as the certification/attestation write (atomic)', but the actual write path has no multi-event batch API — each append_event call acquires write_lock, commits one event, and releases the lock. The SIGNATURE event and ENQUEUE_ENRICH would be two sequential locked commits, not one atomic unit. The fallback ('drain treats certified with stale/no digest as implicitly enqueable') is the real safety net, but the plan presents the unachievable atomicity as the primary path, which will mislead implementers into searching for a non-existent batch write facility.
findings.location: What section: 'Enqueue is written in the SAME locked batch as the certification/attestation write (atomic — no certified-but-unqueued gap; if batching is impossible...)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify whether `stage_and_commit` (or a new batch variant) can group the SIGNATURE and ENQUEUE_ENRICH events under a single `write_lock` hold; if not, scope the required multi-event batch primitive explicitly, or downgrade the atomicity claim to 'best-effort with drain-side recovery' and remove the 'SAME locked batch' language.
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'ENQUEUE_ENRICH is written in the SAME locked batch as the certification/attestation write' for atomicity, but the existing `stage_and_commit` API acquires and releases `write_lock` for exactly one event per call with no batch/multi-event facility. There is no mechanism in `event_append.py` for grouping two events under a single lock hold, so the 'same locked batch' claim is unverified against the actual store API.
findings.location: ## What — 'Enqueue is written in the SAME locked batch as the certification/attestation write'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm whether SOAK and LEASE_TTL should live in an existing section (e.g., `[verify]` alongside `remediation_window_minutes`, or a new `[enrich]` section) rather than the non-existent `[overlap]` section, and register the chosen section in `_config_schema.py` `_SECTION_CLASSES` + `_SECTIONS`.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a new `[overlap]` config section for SOAK and LEASE_TTL, but no such section exists in `_config_schema.py`. The existing config schema has sections: verify, ticket, ticket_clarity, compact, sync, mcp, reconciler, jira, scratch, tracker. Adding a brand-new section for exactly two timing knobs proliferates config surface unnecessarily, when comparable timing parameters (e.g., `remediation_window_minutes` in `[verify]`, `lock_lease_secs` in `[reconciler]`, `COMPACTION_HORIZON_NS` in `[compact]`) already live in existing sections.
findings.location: ## Scope (exact edit targets) — 'Config: `SOAK` and `LEASE_TTL` in the typed `overlap` config section (`_config_schema.py`)'
findings.suggested_fix: Place `soak_minutes` (default 60) and `lease_ttl_minutes` (default 15) in `VerifyConfig` alongside the existing `remediation_window_minutes` (same plan-review timing family), or create a new `[enrich]` section if isolation is preferred — but name it explicitly and register it in `_SECTION_CLASSES`/`_SECTIONS`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify that the `overlap` config section in `_config_schema.py` does not yet exist and must be created (new `@dataclass class OverlapConfig`, added to `Config`, `_SECTION_CLASSES`, and `_SECTIONS`), not merely extended.
findings.finding: The plan instructs placing SOAK and LEASE_TTL config in 'the typed overlap config section (_config_schema.py)', but no 'overlap' section exists in that file. The exhaustive section list is: verify, ticket, ticket_clarity, compact, sync, mcp, reconciler, jira, scratch, tracker — confirmed by reading _config_schema.py in full. An 'overlap' dataclass, _SECTION_CLASSES entry, and _SECTIONS coercion table entry all need to be created as new work, yet the plan writes as if the section already exists.
findings.location: Scope section: 'Config: SOAK and LEASE_TTL in the typed overlap config section (_config_schema.py)'
findings.suggested_fix: Change 'in the typed overlap config section (_config_schema.py)' to 'in a new typed overlap config section (_config_schema.py) — add @dataclass OverlapConfig with soak_minutes: int = 60 and lease_ttl_minutes: int = 15, register it in Config, _SECTION_CLASSES, and _SECTIONS'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Rename or correct the config section target: define a new `EnrichConfig` dataclass (or add fields to an existing section such as `verify`) and register it in `_SECTION_CLASSES`, `_SECTIONS`, and `Config` — there is no existing `overlap` section in `_config_schema.py`.
findings.finding: The plan targets a non-existent `overlap` config section in `_config_schema.py` for `SOAK` and `LEASE_TTL`. No such section exists; the real sections are verify, ticket, ticket_clarity, compact, sync, mcp, reconciler, jira, scratch, tracker. The plan would need to either create a new section (e.g. `enrich`) with a new dataclass + coercers + `_SECTION_CLASSES` / `_SECTIONS` entries, or add keys to an existing section.
findings.location: Scope section: 'Config: `SOAK` and `LEASE_TTL` in the typed `overlap` config section (`_config_schema.py`)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add an `EnrichConfig` dataclass with `soak_minutes: int = 60` and `lease_ttl_minutes: int = 15`, register it in `_SECTION_CLASSES` as `"enrich": EnrichConfig`, in `_SECTIONS` with matching coercers, and add `enrich: EnrichConfig = field(default_factory=EnrichConfig)` to the `Config` dataclass.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the CORRECT existing config section for SOAK and LEASE_TTL (e.g. `verify` or a new `enrich` section added to `_SECTION_CLASSES` and `_SECTIONS`) — the named `overlap` section does not exist in `_config_schema.py`.
findings.finding: The plan directs adding SOAK and LEASE_TTL to the 'typed overlap config section (_config_schema.py)', but no 'overlap' section exists in the schema — the registered sections are verify, ticket, ticket_clarity, compact, sync, mcp, reconciler, jira, scratch, tracker. Adding keys to a non-existent section will trigger _warn_unknown deprecation warnings on every load, or a hard ConfigError with REBAR_CONFIG_UNKNOWN_KEYS=error.
findings.location: Scope section: 'Config: SOAK and LEASE_TTL in the typed overlap config section (_config_schema.py)'
findings.suggested_fix: Either (a) add SOAK (e.g. enrich_soak_minutes: int = 60) and LEASE_TTL (e.g. enrich_lease_ttl_minutes: int = 15) fields to VerifyConfig and the corresponding 'verify' coercion table in _SECTIONS, OR (b) introduce a new EnrichConfig dataclass and register it in _SECTION_CLASSES/_SECTIONS as 'enrich'. Update the plan's Scope section to name the correct section.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the config section name from `overlap` to an existing section (e.g. `verify` or a new explicitly-scoped `enrich` section), and add the required dataclass + `_SECTION_CLASSES`/`_SECTIONS`/`Config` registration to the edit targets if a new section is needed.
findings.finding: The plan places `SOAK` and `LEASE_TTL` config keys in a 'typed overlap config section' of `_config_schema.py`, but no `overlap` section exists in the schema — the actual sections are `verify`, `ticket`, `ticket_clarity`, `compact`, `sync`, `mcp`, `reconciler`, `jira`, `scratch`, `tracker`. Adding an unknown section name would be silently ignored (or hard-errored with `strict=True`) by the existing `coerce_sparse` logic.
findings.location: ## Scope — 'Config: SOAK and LEASE_TTL in the typed overlap config section (_config_schema.py)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add `SOAK_MINUTES` and `LEASE_TTL_MINUTES` to `VerifyConfig` (alongside the existing enrichment-related verify keys) and register them in `_SECTIONS['verify']`, matching the pattern used by `remediation_window_minutes`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the config section name: either use an existing section (e.g. `verify`) or name the new `OverlapConfig` dataclass, its `_SECTION_CLASSES` entry, and its `_SECTIONS` coercer table entries as explicit edit targets in the Scope section.
findings.finding: The plan directs adding SOAK and LEASE_TTL config keys to 'the typed overlap config section (_config_schema.py)', but no 'overlap' section exists in _config_schema.py. The actual sections are: verify, ticket, ticket_clarity, compact, sync, mcp, reconciler, jira, scratch, tracker. Adding to a non-existent section either silently fails (unknown-section warning) or requires creating a new dataclass + coercion table — unspecified work the plan omits.
findings.location: Scope section: 'Config: SOAK and LEASE_TTL in the typed overlap config section (_config_schema.py)'
findings.suggested_fix: Change to: 'Config: add `SOAK_MINUTES: int = 60` and `LEASE_TTL_MINUTES: int = 15` to `VerifyConfig` in `_config_schema.py` (with matching coercers in `_SECTIONS["verify"]`), or add a new `[enrich]` section with a new `EnrichConfig` dataclass — and name the full `_config_schema.py` additions as explicit edit targets.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the new config section for enrichment queue tunables: specify the dataclass name (e.g. `EnrichConfig`), the TOML section key (e.g. `[tool.rebar.enrich]`), field names (`soak_minutes`/`lease_ttl_minutes` or nanosecond variants), default values, coercers, and register it in `_SECTION_CLASSES` / `_SECTIONS` in `_config_schema.py`.
findings.finding: The plan places SOAK and LEASE_TTL in 'the typed overlap config section (_config_schema.py)', but no 'overlap' config section exists in _config_schema.py — there is no OverlapConfig dataclass, no 'overlap' key in _SECTION_CLASSES or _SECTIONS. An implementer must invent the section name, class shape, TOML key, and coercers from scratch.
findings.location: Scope section: 'Config: SOAK and LEASE_TTL in the typed overlap config section (_config_schema.py)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Scope section: 'Add `EnrichConfig(soak_minutes: int = 60, lease_ttl_minutes: int = 15)` dataclass to `_config_schema.py`; register under TOML key `enrich` in `_SECTION_CLASSES` and `_SECTIONS` with `_as_int(v, k, minimum=1)` coercers for both fields.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Name the concrete prune path for queue events (e.g. `src/rebar/llm/enrich_queue/sidecar.py::prune_queue_events(ticket_id, ...)`) and specify the retention policy (e.g. 'after DONE_ENRICH, remove all prior ENQUEUE_ENRICH/CLAIM_ENRICH/DONE_ENRICH for that cycle, keeping the last N cycles').
findings.finding: The prune path for enrichment queue events is described as 'name the prune path' — the plan explicitly defers naming the concrete module/function responsible for bounding ENQUEUE_ENRICH / CLAIM_ENRICH / DONE_ENRICH growth. Unlike REVIEW_RESULT (which has a named prune function in src/rebar/llm/plan_review/sidecar.py::prune), no equivalent is named for the queue events.
findings.location: Scope section: 'Log-growth note: ... a bounded retention/prune keeps a ticket's queue events to the latest cycle (name the prune path)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the prune path: define when and how queue events are compacted after `DONE_ENRICH` (e.g., retain only the latest ENQUEUE/CLAIM/DONE triple per ticket; prune triggered on drain completion or a dedicated compact step), and add an AC verifying the reducer's event scan is bounded.
findings.finding: The prune path for queue-event log growth is left as an open placeholder ('name the prune path') without specifying how or when pruning occurs. The `pending_enrichment` reducer must scan all accumulated ENQUEUE_ENRICH / CLAIM_ENRICH / DONE_ENRICH events per ticket to compute state; with no prune, this is an unbounded O(event-history) scan per drain cycle.
findings.location: ## Scope — 'Log-growth note: … the drain appends DONE_ENRICH and a bounded retention/prune keeps a ticket's queue events to the latest cycle (name the prune path).'
findings.suggested_fix: After each successful `DONE_ENRICH` append, emit a compaction pass that deletes all prior ENQUEUE_ENRICH / CLAIM_ENRICH events for that ticket (keeping only the terminal DONE_ENRICH). Document this as the prune contract and add a test `test_prune_after_done` asserting the event count per ticket stays ≤ a small constant after N enrichment cycles.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace '(name the prune path)' with a concrete decision: name the prune function/module (e.g. `src/rebar/_store/enrich_prune.py::prune_enrich_events`), state when it runs (e.g. called by the drain runner after DONE_ENRICH), and what it retains (e.g. last DONE_ENRICH + last ENQUEUE_ENRICH per ticket).
findings.finding: The Scope section says 'a bounded retention/prune keeps a ticket's queue events to the latest cycle (name the prune path)' — 'name the prune path' is an explicit placeholder deferring a real design decision (what module/function handles pruning, when it runs, what it prunes) to the executor.
findings.location: Scope section — 'Log-growth note' bullet

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the prune path concretely: name the module/function, define the trigger (e.g., post-DONE_ENRICH in the same locked batch, or a periodic compaction job), and state the retention policy (e.g., keep only the latest ENQUEUE/CLAIM/DONE cycle per ticket_id).
findings.finding: The retire/prune path for sidecar queue events is acknowledged but never actually specified — the plan says 'name the prune path' as a parenthetical instruction rather than defining what the path is, who owns it, when it runs, or what its retention policy is. Shared event state with no defined retirement path leaks/accumulates indefinitely.
findings.location: Scope section — 'Log-growth note: these sidecar events accumulate like REVIEW_RESULT; the drain appends DONE_ENRICH and a bounded retention/prune keeps a ticket's queue events to the latest cycle (name the prune path).'
findings.suggested_fix: After appending DONE_ENRICH, in the same write_lock batch, append or overwrite a compaction marker that allows a background sweep to delete all earlier queue events for that ticket_id. Add a `prune_queue_events(ticket_id)` function in `src/rebar/_store/event_append.py` called by the drain runner (S4) after DONE_ENRICH is confirmed.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Make the growth-bounding sub-criterion of AC5 measurable: name the prune function, state the retention limit (e.g. 'keep only the last ENQUEUE_ENRICH and last DONE_ENRICH per ticket after a DONE_ENRICH is appended'), and add a proving test node (e.g. `::test_prune_bounds_event_count`).
findings.finding: AC5 states 'queue-event growth is bounded by the named prune path' but the prune path is unnamed (see E2 finding above), making this criterion unmeasurable — there is no concrete observable outcome or proving command that could confirm boundedness.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC5

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an integration-level AC (e.g., `tests/integration/test_claim_race_live.py`) that exercises `test_claim_race` with a real on-disk store and two competing threads (or subprocesses) so that `fcntl.flock(LOCK_EX)` contention is actually exercised — not just an in-memory/mocked substitute.
findings.finding: The `test_claim_race` unit test may proxy-validate the file-lock atomicity boundary: a single-process unit test cannot reliably exercise actual `fcntl.flock(LOCK_EX)` contention between two drainers. The plan places this test in `tests/unit/` with no mention of threads sharing a real file descriptor or subprocesses, meaning the 'exactly one CLAIM_ENRICH lands' assertion could pass even if the real lock is never held — e.g., via an in-memory or monkeypatched store. The correctness guarantee ('read-check-then-append INSIDE the write lock') depends entirely on the OS-level exclusive lock; a unit test that bypasses that boundary lets the suite go green while the live path is untested.
findings.location: Testing section / AC line `::test_claim_race` / 'Claim correctness' section
findings.suggested_fix: Add an AC: 'A live integration test spawns two threads (or subprocesses) sharing a real temporary on-disk event store and races them to claim the same ticket; assert exactly one `CLAIM_ENRICH` event is appended. This test may run under `pytest -m integration` and is not required to be fast.' This ensures the `fcntl.flock` boundary is exercised rather than mocked away.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add the three new event types (`ENQUEUE_ENRICH`, `CLAIM_ENRICH`, `DONE_ENRICH`) to the `StoreError` message string in `stage_and_commit` (lines 97–103 of `event_append.py`) when updating `EVENT_TYPES`.
findings.finding: The hardcoded error-message string in `event_append.py` (lines 97–102) exhaustively lists valid event types by name and must be updated when the three new types are added to `EVENT_TYPES`. This is an unenumerated consumer inside the same file: the frozenset and the error string can silently diverge, producing a misleading 'Must be one of: …' message that omits the new types.
findings.location: `src/rebar/_store/event_append.py` lines 97–103 — `StoreError` message string inside `stage_and_commit`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that `test_claim_race` uses real OS threads (or subprocesses) with the real `write_lock` flock — not a mocked lock — or document a rationale for why a mock is sufficient (e.g. the lock is integration-tested elsewhere).
findings.finding: AC3's proving command (`::test_claim_race`) is labeled a 'unit' test but must exercise real fcntl.flock concurrency (two concurrent claimers). A unit test that mocks or monkeypatches the lock cannot prove the exclusive-lock claim race is correctly handled — the mock bypasses the very behavior under test (fixture invalidity, defect taxonomy #4).
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC3; Testing section
findings.suggested_fix: In the Testing section, change the `test_claim_race` description to: 'spawn two threads, each calling the real claim path (real flock on a tmp file); assert exactly one CLAIM_ENRICH appended and the other thread returns a no-op; no mocking of write_lock'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete proving command to AC4, e.g. 'verified by `pytest tests/unit/test_enrich_queue.py::test_lease_expiry_reclaimable`', and add the corresponding test case to the Testing section.
findings.finding: AC4 ('A claim with lease_expires_ns < now is reclaimable on the next drain; re-processing is idempotent') names no proving command or test node, unlike ACs 1–3 which each cite a specific pytest node. It is a prose assertion with no verification path.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC4
findings.suggested_fix: Append 'verified by `pytest tests/unit/test_enrich_queue.py::test_lease_expiry_reclaimable`' to AC4, and add `test_lease_expiry_reclaimable` (sets lease_expires_ns in the past, runs drain loop, asserts new CLAIM_ENRICH appended) and `test_done_idempotent` to the unit test list.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a note in Scope or Dependencies naming the existing cert-path callers and any currently-passing tests that exercise the locked batch, and confirm their behavior is unchanged (or describe the expected delta).
findings.finding: The plan inserts an enqueue call into the existing cert-path write ('SAME locked batch as the certification/attestation write') but does not name or acknowledge existing callers of that path, existing tests that exercise it, or what observable behavior changes for those callers (e.g. the locked batch now also appends a sidecar event, enlarging the batch and extending lock hold time).
findings.location: ## What — 'Enqueue is written in the SAME locked batch as the certification/attestation write'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Name the prune path for queue events (ENQUEUE_ENRICH/CLAIM_ENRICH/DONE_ENRICH), assert it is rollback-safe (does not delete DONE_ENRICH markers a rolled-back binary cannot re-emit), and add a post-rollback schema-state assertion (e.g. 'on rollback: existing queue events are preserved-and-ignored; the prune does not touch DONE_ENRICH files; re-deploy resumes without re-draining completed tickets').
findings.finding: The plan leaves the prune path for queue events unnamed ('name the prune path' appears as an open TO-DO) and makes no assertion about the store state after a binary rollback. On rollback: (1) the three event types leave their files on disk (preserved-and-ignored, safe), but (2) if the un-named prune is implemented as a post-DONE_ENRICH cleanup that a rolled-back binary can accidentally trigger (e.g. via a catch-all or REVIEW_RESULT-style prune), CLAIM_ENRICH/DONE_ENRICH markers could be deleted, making the drain re-process already-completed tickets on re-deploy. The plan does not assert the post-rollback schema state (required by T11(e)) — it only names rollback as a non-issue by omission.
findings.location: ## Scope — 'Log-growth note: … bounded retention/prune keeps a ticket's queue events to the latest cycle (name the prune path)'; ## Acceptance Criteria — AC5 '… queue-event growth is bounded by the named prune path'
findings.suggested_fix: In the Scope section, name the prune path explicitly (e.g. 'prune path: after appending DONE_ENRICH, prune ENQUEUE_ENRICH and CLAIM_ENRICH events for that ticket's completed cycle, analogous to sidecar.py:prune, but explicitly exclude DONE_ENRICH files from deletion so they remain as idempotency guards'). Add a rollback note: 'On binary rollback: the three event types remain on disk as preserved-and-ignored files; the old binary's prune does not know these types and will not touch them (type-suffixed filenames ensure no false match); drain is halted on rollback hosts (StoreError on append); re-deploy resumes from the surviving DONE_ENRICH state with no re-processing.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the analogy: TICKET_DIGEST does not yet exist in the codebase (it is the subject of dependency S2); state explicitly that REVIEW_RESULT is the sole existing precedent and that TICKET_DIGEST is a forthcoming parallel pattern contingent on S2 landing first.
findings.finding: The plan parenthetically describes the three new event types as 'like REVIEW_RESULT/TICKET_DIGEST' to establish the sidecar pattern, implying TICKET_DIGEST already exists as an established, mirrored precedent. However, a full search of src/rebar finds zero occurrences of TICKET_DIGEST in any source file: it is NOT in EVENT_TYPES, NOT in _NON_REPLAY_KNOWN_TYPES, and NOT in any reducer or sidecar module. TICKET_DIGEST is the subject of dependency S2 (wispy-cheerless-tick) — a currently open story that has not yet landed.
findings.location: Scope section: 'add them to _NON_REPLAY_KNOWN_TYPES (sidecar, preserved-and-ignored; like REVIEW_RESULT/TICKET_DIGEST)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a failure fallback for the CLAIM_ENRICH append step: specify what the drain does when `stage_and_commit` raises (e.g. 'on StoreError/LockTimeout: log a warning, skip this ticket for the current drain run, and rely on lease-expiry for recovery on the next run').
findings.finding: No fallback is specified for a write failure (StoreError / LockTimeout) during the locked CLAIM_ENRICH append itself. The plan covers the concurrency case ('loser picks another ticket') but not the case where the winning claimer's append throws — leaving the drain in an ambiguous state with a held conceptual claim that was never persisted.
findings.location: Claim correctness section: 'while holding write_lock, re-reduce ... if still unclaimed/expired, append CLAIM_ENRICH; else abort and pick another ticket'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Claim correctness: 'On StoreError or LockTimeout during the CLAIM_ENRICH append: log at WARNING level, treat the ticket as unclaimed for this drain run, and continue to the next candidate. The self-healing reaper recovers on the next drain pass.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the title/body conflict: change the title's 'optimistic claim' to 'lock-guarded claim' (or whatever the intended term is) so title and body agree on the claim mechanism.
findings.finding: The plan title ends with 'optimistic claim' but the body describes a pessimistic, exclusive-lock mechanism (read-check-then-append inside fcntl LOCK_EX). These terms mean opposite things; an implementer reading the title may reach for CAS/version-check patterns instead of the lock-guarded approach the body specifies.
findings.location: Title vs. 'Claim correctness' section
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'optimistic claim' in the title with 'lock-guarded claim' to match the body's exclusive-flock description.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (or clarify the dependency section) specifying exactly where/how the S2 digest freshness signal is consulted — e.g. whether it gates enqueue, gates drain claim, or is only used by the drain runner (S4) — and add a corresponding test case.
findings.finding: The S2 dependency is declared as providing a 'digest freshness signal the enqueue/drain consult,' but no AC or test covers whether/how the enqueue or drain actually consults this signal. All ACs test cert+SOAK mechanics only, leaving the S2 integration behavior unverified and potentially orphaned.
findings.location: Dependencies section; What section (enqueue description); AC1–AC5
findings.suggested_fix: If digest consultation belongs to the drain runner (S4), remove S2 from this story's Dependencies and note it as an S4 concern. If it gates enqueue or claim here, add an explicit AC such as: 'Enqueue is suppressed / claim is skipped when the ticket has no fresh digest (per S2 signal); verified by ::test_digest_freshness_gate'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC and unit test for the fallback path: when a certification exists with no corresponding ENQUEUE_ENRICH event, the drain (or a scan step) treats it as implicitly enqueable.
findings.finding: The fallback behavior 'if batching is impossible, the drain treats certified-with-stale/no-digest as implicitly enqueable' is described in the What section as a correctness guarantee but has no covering acceptance criterion or test.
findings.location: What section (Enqueue bullet, parenthetical fallback); AC1–AC5

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an ADR or CLAUDE.md section documenting the 'event store as queue' architectural decision: why a dedicated broker was rejected, the sidecar-event invariants (latest-wins, lock-guarded claim, soak-as-stored-timestamp), and the prune contract — referenced from the plan's Scope section.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a novel architectural pattern — using the event store as a durable queue substrate — but names no ADR, CLAUDE.md entry, or design-doc update to capture this decision. Future maintainers (or a drain-runner implementer) have no canonical document explaining why a bolted-on broker was rejected, what invariants the sidecar-event model relies on, or what the prune contract is.
findings.location: ## Why / ## Scope (exact edit targets)
findings.suggested_fix: Create `docs/adr/NNN-enrichment-queue.md` (or append to CLAUDE.md) covering: (1) rationale for event-store-as-queue, (2) the three sidecar event types and their reduce semantics, (3) the lock-guarded claim protocol, (4) the prune contract for `DONE_ENRICH` retention. Reference it in the plan's Scope section.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add observability requirements: specify at minimum a structured log line for each of ENQUEUE_ENRICH written, CLAIM_ENRICH won/lost, lease expiry reclaimation, and DONE_ENRICH (with ticket_id and timestamps), and name the log site in the relevant source file or AC.
findings.finding: No observability is specified for any of the new failure points introduced by this plan: ENQUEUE_ENRICH write failures, CLAIM_ENRICH race losers, lease expirations/reclaimations, or prune execution. Operators cannot detect a stuck queue, a runaway lease-expiry loop, or a prune that never fires.
findings.location: Entire plan — no logging, metrics, or alerting is mentioned for any queue operation.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile the plan title with the H1 and body: replace 'optimistic claim' in the document subtitle/title line with 'lock-guarded claim' (or add a note explaining why 'optimistic' was historically used and that it means lock-guarded-append here).
findings.finding: The document title line says 'optimistic claim' while the H1 heading and the body both describe a PESSIMISTIC, exclusive-lock-guarded claim (read-check-then-append inside write_lock). These are fundamentally different concurrency strategies. A reader or future agent acting on the title will implement the wrong mechanism.
findings.location: Document title line ('optimistic claim') vs H1 heading ('lock-guarded claim') vs 'Claim correctness' section
findings.suggested_fix: Change the subtitle to 'lock-guarded claim' everywhere it appears, matching the H1 and the body's explicit description of the exclusive-lock read-check-then-append mechanism.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm that `pending_enrichment` is intentionally shipped as a 'ready for S4' building block with no current caller, or scope it as an internal helper tested only via the unit suite until S4 lands and calls it.
findings.finding: The `pending_enrichment(now_ns)` reducer projection is introduced in this story, but its only consumer — the drain runner — is explicitly scoped OUT ("OUT: the drain runner (S4)"). At delivery time, the projection has zero active call-sites, making it a premature abstraction under the Rule-of-Three criterion.
findings.location: ## Scope (exact edit targets) — reducer projection; ## Scope — 'OUT: the drain runner (S4)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the `drainer_id` value vocabulary in the CLAIM_ENRICH body definition (e.g. 'a UUID4 string generated once per drain process invocation, used as an audit/tracing key; not used in reclaimability logic').
findings.finding: The CLAIM_ENRICH body includes a `drainer_id` field, but the plan never defines what value a drainer_id takes — its format, source, or uniqueness guarantees are unspecified. This matters for the lease-expiry/reclaimability decision rule and for implementing test_claim_race.
findings.location: What section: 'CLAIM_ENRICH (body: {ticket_id, drainer_id, lease_expires_ns})'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
